## Cachelane Codebase Exploration Report

I've completed a thorough examination of the Cachelane codebase. Here's the current state after M3 (commit 6f15017):

### 1. Top-Level Repository Structure

```
/Users/jimmy/Documents/CacheLane/
├── src/                         # Source code (TypeScript)
├── designs/                     # Implementation-ready specs (MD files + diagrams)
├── docs/                        # Supplementary documentation
├── .github/                     # CI/CD workflows
├── package.json                 # Node 20.10+, deps listed below
├── tsconfig.json                # ES2022 target, strict mode
├── tsup.config.ts               # Dual ESM/CJS build config
├── vitest.config.ts             # Unit/integration test config
├── eslint.config.js             # Linting (includes no-restricted-paths for module layering)
└── CLAUDE.md                    # Critical implementation invariants & workflow
```

### 2. Source Code Layout (`src/`)

The codebase is organized into **6 modules** with strict downward-only layering:

```
src/
├── types/                       # Core types (Volatility, BlockKind, Block, PrefixState, CachelaneConfig)
│   ├── index.ts
│   └── __tests__/types.test.ts
├── config/                      # Configuration management
│   ├── index.ts
│   ├── defaults.ts             # DEFAULT_CONFIG with pruner/keepalive/classification settings
│   └── __tests__/config.test.ts
├── storage/                     # SQLite persistence layer
│   ├── index.ts                # openDatabase() factory, CachelaneDb interface
│   ├── migrations/
│   │   └── 001_initial.sql     # Schema: blocks, turns, block_references tables
│   └── __tests__/storage.test.ts
├── tokenizer/                   # Token counting per model
│   ├── index.ts                # countTokens(), cached via model-table
│   ├── model-table.ts          # Per-model multipliers (4.6 vs 4.7 support)
│   └── __tests__/tokenizer.test.ts
├── classifier/                  # M2 — block classification (all 11 BlockKind values)
│   ├── index.ts                # classifyBlock(), classifyBlocks()
│   ├── types.ts                # UnclassifiedBlock, Classification, ClassifierConfig
│   ├── rules.ts                # Rule engine for BlockKind detection
│   ├── volatility-map.ts       # KIND_TO_VOLATILITY & PIN_VOLATILITY mappings
│   ├── glob.ts                 # Pattern matching for pin/exclude rules
│   └── __tests__/
│       ├── classifier.test.ts  # Main classification tests
│       ├── glob.test.ts
│       └── fixtures/           # 11 JSON fixtures (one per BlockKind)
├── orchestrator/               # M3 — core cache orchestration
│   ├── index.ts                # orchestrate() entry point (main hot path)
│   ├── types.ts                # OrchestratorInput, MutatedRequest, Breakpoints
│   ├── reorderer.ts            # reorder() — finds region boundaries (prefix/middle/suffix)
│   ├── breakpoint-placer.ts    # placeBreakpoints() — SHA-256 hashing + state tracking
│   ├── request-mutator.ts      # mutateRequest() — adds cache_control markers
│   ├── cache-state-tracker.ts  # CacheStateTracker class — in-memory per-workspace state
│   └── __tests__/
│       ├── orchestrator.test.ts
│       ├── reorderer.test.ts
│       ├── breakpoint-placer.test.ts
│       ├── cache-state-tracker.test.ts
│       ├── cache-stability.test.ts     # Cache-stability gate (M3 requirement)
│       ├── request-mutator.test.ts
│       └── fixtures/
└── index.ts                    # Public exports (all modules)
```

### 3. What M3 Delivered (Merged commit 6f15017)

M3 implements the **orchestrator core** with:

- **Reorderer** (`reorderer.ts`): Scans classifications to find the boundary between SEMI/STABLE messages and the first VOLATILE message. Returns `RegionBoundaries.middle_end_in_messages`.

- **Breakpoint Placer** (`breakpoint-placer.ts`): 
  - Computes SHA-256 of prefix (system + tools)
  - Computes SHA-256 of middle region (SEMI/STABLE messages)
  - Includes middle breakpoint only if the middle hash matches the previous turn's middle hash

- **Request Mutator** (`request-mutator.ts`):
  - Adds `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral", ttl: "5m" }` marker to last tool or system block (prefix breakpoint)
  - Adds same marker to last content item of the last SEMI message (middle breakpoint, if conditions are met)

- **Cache State Tracker** (`cache-state-tracker.ts`): 
  - In-memory `Map<workspace_id, PrefixState>` tracking hash, TTL class, timestamps, and expiry
  - No persistence yet (M4+ adds to SQLite)

- **Cache-Stability Gate**: Tests verify SHA-256 of prefix region is byte-identical across 3 consecutive identical-input runs (this is a **merge blocker** per CLAUDE.md).

### 4. Database Schema (Already in Place)

SQLite schema is defined in `src/storage/migrations/001_initial.sql`:

| Table | Purpose | Key Columns |
|-------|---------|------------|
| `blocks` | Per-block metadata | `id, workspace_id, session_id, kind, volatility, is_pinned, token_count, added_at_turn, last_referenced_at_turn, unused_turns, is_stub, stub_summary, refetch_handle` |

30	## Detail
31	
32	### Q001 — Reference-Detection Precision & Recall
33	
34	**Question:** Does the three-signal deterministic detector (file paths in tool calls + block IDs in
35	text + 40-char shingle overlap) achieve ≥ 95% precision and ≥ 85% recall against real Claude Code
36	sessions?
37	
38	**Why it matters:** If precision is low, too many blocks are incorrectly marked "referenced" and
39	K-pruning saves fewer tokens. If recall is low, blocks are stubbed when they shouldn't be, causing
40	unnecessary refetch round-trips.
41	
42	**Resolution path:**
43	1. Build a 100-session annotated corpus (6-hour human task — **must start during M2 or M3**)
44	2. Run the three-signal detector against the corpus
45	3. Measure precision and recall; CI gate asserts ≥ 95% / ≥ 85%
46	4. Only after this gate passes does M4 proceed
47	
48	**Blocks:** M4 (Reference detection milestone). The corpus must exist in-repo before pruner code
49	is written (AC-6).
50	
51	**Owner:** Lead engineer  
52	**Target:** 2 weeks after Phase 2 approval
53	
54	---
55	
56	### Q002 — Classifier Accuracy
57	
58	**Question:** Does the fingerprint-based conservative classifier (defaults to `VOLATILE` unless
59	file paths + last-modified mtimes match a stable signature) correctly classify real-world prompt
60	blocks?
61	
62	**Resolution path:** Validate against the same 100-session corpus as Q001 (parallel work).
63	Initial allowlist (CLAUDE.md, tool schemas, system prompt, pinned files) is a starting point.
64	
65	**Owner:** Lead engineer  
66	**Target:** 2 weeks (parallel with Q001)

71	
72	**Question:** Is K=3 the right default? What is the optimal K per scenario type?
73	
74	**Resolution path:** Experiment K ∈ {2, 3, 4, 5, 6} across the 5 benchmark scenarios (§2.4.5 of
75	Phase 2 spec). Measure net effective cost = effective input cost + keepalive overhead. Paired
76	Wilcoxon signed-rank vs. A1 baseline.
77	
78	**Current defaults pending experiment:** K=3 (default), K=2 (aggressive), K=5 (conservative).
79

16	
17	| ID | Requirement | Source |

20	| REQ-F-002 | The orchestrator SHALL read Anthropic API usage fields on every turn: `cache_creation_input_tokens`, `cache_read_input_tokens`, `ephemeral_5m_input_tokens`, `ephemeral_1h_input_tokens` via `@anthropic-ai/sdk`. | §1.1 |
21	| REQ-F-003 | Block-size accounting SHALL use `@anthropic-ai/tokenizer` with a **model-string lookup** to select the correct tokenizer per request (supports both Opus 4.6 and 4.7). | §1.1, Q8 |
22	| REQ-F-004 | Persistent storage SHALL be a single-file SQLite database via `better-sqlite3` in WAL mode. No external store. | §1.1, §1.2.2, D5 |
23	| REQ-F-005 | In-memory cache-state tracking SHALL live in the orchestrator process using plain `Map`/`Set`; restart recovery relies on the on-disk SQLite log as the authoritative source. | §1.1 |
24	| REQ-F-006 | Hashing for prefix fingerprinting and block deduplication SHALL use SHA-256 via `node:crypto`. | §1.1 |
25	| REQ-F-007 | The CLI SHALL be implemented with `commander`. | §1.1 |
26	| REQ-F-008 | Logging SHALL be structured JSON via `pino`, with `pino-pretty` in dev. Log lines SHALL include billable usage fields. | §1.1, §1.4 |
27	| REQ-F-009 | Local logs SHALL be written to `~/.cachelane/logs/`, **rotated daily**, with **7-day retention**. | §1.4 |
28	| REQ-F-010 | The system SHALL expose a `cachelane stats` CLI command that reads directly from the SQLite log (no external metrics service). | §1.4 |

31	
32	| ID | Requirement | Source |
33	|----|-------------|--------|
34	| REQ-F-011 | The system SHALL implement opt-in anonymous telemetry reporting cache-hit ratio, average reduction, and model used; **default OFF**; permanently disable-able. | §1.4 |
35	| REQ-F-012 | Telemetry MUST NOT be enabled by default; activation requires an explicit command. | §1.4, Q7, D9 |
36	| REQ-F-013 | All processing SHALL occur locally. No request bodies, prompts, or completions may leave the user's machine except in the direct path to `api.anthropic.com`. | §1.5, D7 |
37	| REQ-F-014 | The reference log SHALL store **block IDs, classifications, token counts, usage counters only** — NOT block contents. | §1.5 |
38	| REQ-F-015 | Block contents SHALL live only transiently in the orchestrator process and SHALL NOT be persisted. | §1.5 |
39	| REQ-F-016 | The Anthropic API key SHALL be read from the user's existing Claude Code config; Cachelane MUST NOT read, store, or forward it outside the originating API call. | §1.5 |
40	| REQ-F-017 | The system SHALL respect Anthropic workspace-level cache isolation (effective since Feb 2026): Cachelane MUST use Claude Code's existing workspace ID and MUST NOT share prefixes across workspaces. | §1.5, Q6 |
41	| REQ-F-018 | The reference log SHALL be keyed by workspace ID. | Q6 |
42	| REQ-F-019 | The system SHALL implement a synthetic **keepalive ping** mechanism: a minimal API call (`max_tokens=1`, one-token user message) that touches the same prefix to reset its TTL. | §2.1 |
43	| REQ-F-020 | Keepalive pings MUST NOT fire when a real turn just touched the cache (idle-only triggering). | §2.2, B1 |

50	| REQ-F-022 | A `cachelane.config.json` (`~/.cachelane/config.json`) SHALL expose manual override knobs for keepalive policy and all other configurable parameters. | §2.4.6 |

121	
122	## API Contracts
123	
124	### C-1: Anthropic Messages API (south-bound, consumed)
125	
126	- **Client SDK:** `@anthropic-ai/sdk`
127	- **Required response fields read per turn:**
128	  - `cache_creation_input_tokens`
129	  - `cache_read_input_tokens`
130	  - `ephemeral_5m_input_tokens`
131	  - `ephemeral_1h_input_tokens`
132	- **Cache semantics:** byte-exact prefix matching with TTL. Workspace-isolated since Feb 2026.
133	- **Pricing:** 0.1× read, 1.25× 5-min write, 2.0× 1-hour write.
134	
135	### C-2: Keepalive Ping Request
136	
137	- **Payload:** one-token user message; `max_tokens=1`; identical prefix to current session
138	- **Effect:** resets the prefix's TTL
139	- **Per-ping billable cost (model):** ~`0.1 × P + 5` base-input-token units
140	
141	### C-3: MCP Server (Cachelane ↔ Claude Code)
142	
143	- **Transport:** stdio (no network port)
144	- **SDK:** `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` ^1.x
145	- **Host requirement:** Claude Code ≥ 0.6
146	- **MCP tools exposed:** `cachelane:stats`, `cachelane:explain`, `cachelane:expand`
147	
148	### C-4: Telemetry Endpoint (opt-in, operated by project)
149	
150	- **URL:** `https://telemetry.cachelane.dev/v1/report`
151	- **Default state:** OFF
152	- **Payload fields (allowed):** `installation_id` (random UUID), `version`, `reporting_period_ms`, `turns`, `cache_hit_ratio`, `pruner_enabled`, `keepalive_policy`, `effective_cost_units_total`, `no_cachelane_baseline_total`
153	- **Payload fields (forbidden):** `workspace_id`, `session_id`, file paths, block contents, model name, API key, IP-correlatable timestamps
154	- **Rate:** at most once per hour per installation
155	- **Response:** 204
156	
157	### C-5: CLI Surface (commander)
158	
159	```
160	cachelane install
161	cachelane stats [--since <ISO-duration>]
162	cachelane stats --opt-in
163	cachelane explain [--turn <N>]

221	
222	## Per-Turn Overhead Budget
223	
224	All orchestrator work must be invisible relative to the Anthropic API round-trip (~200ms RTT).
225	
226	| Operation | Target |
227	|-----------|--------|
228	| Block decomposition | < 1 ms |
229	| Classification (all blocks) | < 2 ms (O(N), N ≤ 200) |
230	| Storage load (counter SELECT) | < 5 ms |
231	| Pruning | < 1 ms (O(N)) |
232	| Reordering + sort | < 2 ms (O(N log N)) |
233	| Breakpoint placement | < 0.5 ms (constant time) |
234	| **Total PreRequest overhead** | **< 12 ms** (< 6% of 200ms RTT) |
235	| Reference detection (PostResponse) | < 10 ms (100 blocks + 10KB response) |
236	| Storage update (counter UPDATE) | < 5 ms |
237	| **Total PostResponse overhead** | **< 20 ms** (response already streaming — latency-invisible) |
238	
239	---
240	
241	## Memory Budget
242	
243	| Component | Budget |
244	|-----------|--------|
245	| Orchestrator process RSS (steady state) | < 50 MB |
246	| Per-session `SessionState` | ~50 KB (100 blocks × ~500 B metadata) |
247	| Cache state tracker | ~1 KB per workspace |
248	| 10 workspaces × 50-turn sessions | ~30 MB |
249	
250	---
251	
252	## Scalability Model
253	
254	- One Claude Code session = one Cachelane MCP server process (~10 MB each)
255	- 20 concurrent sessions → 20 processes
256	- SQLite WAL mode: concurrent readers + serialised writers; < 5 writes/turn is far below SQLite's limits
257	- No central backend, no autoscaling, no shared state across users
258	
259	**Bottleneck:** Signal 3 of reference detection (40-char shingle, O(B × |text|)) is the most
260	expensive operation — bounded at ~5 ms for typical sessions.
261	
262	---
263	
264	## Reliability & Failure Handling
265	
266	**Core principle: fail open.** "Cachelane never makes Claude Code slower or less reliable than it
267	would have been without Cachelane installed."
268	
269	| Failure | Response |
270	|---------|----------|
271	| Anthropic 5xx | Bubble up unchanged; don't retry |
272	| Anthropic 4xx (bad `cache_control`) | Log full payload (key redacted); bubble up; increment counter |
273	| SQLite write failure | Log; drop counter update for this turn; **continue** |
274	| SQLite read failure | Return defaults (`unused_turns = 0`); continue |
275	| SQLite corruption (startup `PRAGMA integrity_check`) | Rename to `.corrupt-{timestamp}`; create empty DB; warn |
276	| Hook exception | Log stack; return unmutated request; pass-through for one turn |
277	| Keepalive ping fails | Log info; retry next interval; do NOT crash worker |
278	| Reference detection panic | Default to "no references"; increment all counters |
279	| Disk full on log rotation | Log to stderr; skip persistence; continue |
280	| Malformed config JSON | Fall back to defaults; refuse to overwrite user config |
281	| Config schema newer than supported | Refuse to start |
282	| Config schema older | Run migration |
283	| Process restart | Cache state tracker resets; next turn pays one cache-write penalty |
284	
285	**Graceful shutdown:** SIGTERM → stop accepting hooks → finish in-flight → flush storage → exit
286	within 2 seconds.
287	
288	**Self-diagnosis:** `cachelane doctor` checks Node ≥ 20.10, Claude Code ≥ 0.6, hook registration,
289	MCP registration, SQLite writability, config parseability, recent cache-hit ratio (warns if < 0.2
290	sustained over 50 turns — indicates prefix-instability bug).
291	
292	---
293	
294	## Security & Privacy

That's a tautology that reports ~100% recall while the real-world implicit cases (the assistant reasoning about a file without quoting it) silently slip through. Synthetic sessions are genuinely useful as unit/regression fixtures (construct a session where the assistant calls `view auth.py`, assert Signal 1 fires) — and the script supports those — but they cannot be the validity corpus.

What you actually run (the human cost is the one optional `anchor` step, ~1 hour, once):
bash

```bash
# optional: emit residual rows from 15 sessions to hand-label for calibration
tsx scripts/corpus/build-corpus.ts anchor --in ~/.claude/projects --sessions 15
# full automated labeling pass → freezes corpus/sessions/*.json
tsx scripts/corpus/build-corpus.ts build --in ~/.claude/projects --sessions 30
# optional: confirm the judge agrees with your anchor labels (>90% = trust it)
tsx scripts/corpus/build-corpus.ts calibrate --human corpus/anchor.human.json
# run the gate locally
tsx scripts/corpus/eval.ts corpus
```

The three things that keep this honest and lean:

* You don't make sessions — you already have them. Point `--in` at `~/.claude/projects` and it pulls your real M1–M3 build transcripts. No synthetic corpus.
* Most labels cost nothing. The mechanical labeler auto-derives every tool-call and id-mention reference deterministically (the smoke test caught the `auth.py` edit on its own). Only the fuzzy residual goes to the judge, unattended.
* CI stays model-free. The judge is throwaway scaffolding that runs once at labeling time; `eval.ts` runs the deterministic detector against frozen labels. It lives in `scripts/`, never bundles, never ships — your product surface stays exactly as lean as it is now.
Two seams to wire when M4 lands (both marked `SEAM` in the code): swap the fallback `decompose.ts` for `src/orchestrator`'s real `decomposeIntoBlocks`, and point `eval.ts` at the real `src/references/three-signal-detector` (then delete `detector-stub.ts`). The vitest gate just imports `evaluate` + `loadCorpus` and asserts `precision >= 0.95 && recall >= 0.85`.
One thing I'd flag before you lean on the numbers: `eval.ts` prints two recall figures. Overall recall is the spec gate, but watch implicit-only recall — it's computed over judge/human labels only, because grading Signals 1 & 2 against the mechanical labels is the detector grading its own homework. If overall passes but implicit recall is low, your detector is coasting on explicit signals and Signal 3 isn't earning its keep.
And the governance reminder, since it's the same trap M1 fell into: if you drop the corpus from 100 → 30 sessions (statistically defensible — ~30 clears a ±10% CI on 85% recall), that's a real edit to REQ-NF-008/009 and `designs/06`/`07`, not a quiet change. Reconcile the docs and get the second sign-off so it doesn't drift.

67	### M5 — K-Pruner  *(depends on M4)*
68	
69	**Spec:** [designs/06-systems-design.md:365](designs/06-systems-design.md); pruning algorithm + worked examples in [designs/04-turns-and-pruning.md](designs/04-turns-and-pruning.md).
70	
71	**Goal.** Once a block has been idle for K consecutive turns (default K=3), replace it with a tiny stub (ID + one-line summary + refetch handle). If the assistant later needs the real content, it calls `cachelane:expand` and the orchestrator re-injects the full block. Net effect: token count stops growing linearly with session length.
72	
73	**Deliverables.**
74	1. Stub materialisation — replace eligible blocks with `{ id, summary, refetch_handle }` markers.
75	2. Refetch flow — `cachelane:expand` MCP tool that restores full content on demand.
76	3. K thresholds — defaults from [designs/03-engineering-specs.md:184](designs/03-engineering-specs.md): default K=3, aggressive K=2, conservative K=5. Optimal K confirmed empirically in M8 (Q003).
77	
78	**Gate.** Integration test: 6-turn synthetic session; pruning kicks in at turn 4. Worked example in [designs/04-turns-and-pruning.md](designs/04-turns-and-pruning.md).
79	
80	**Cross-cutting concern (Q010).** When a stub is re-expanded on turn N+1, the reorderer must handle the now-VOLATILE expanded block correctly. Flag in [designs/07-open-questions.md](designs/07-open-questions.md) — resolve during M5 implementation.
81	
82	---
83	
84	### M6 — Keepalive Worker  *(depends on M5)*
85	
86	**Spec:** [designs/06-systems-design.md:366](designs/06-systems-design.md); REQ-F-019 through REQ-F-021 at [designs/03-engineering-specs.md:42–49](designs/03-engineering-specs.md).
87	
88	**Goal.** Anthropic's prompt cache expires after 5 minutes of idleness (or 1 hour on the longer TTL). If the user steps away mid-session, the next turn pays the full cache-creation cost again. The keepalive worker fires a synthetic 1-token request (`max_tokens=1`) that touches the same prefix to reset its TTL.
89	
90	**Deliverables.**
91	1. Background async worker — checks TTL expiry every ~10 s.
92	2. Four keepalive policies: `off`, `static`, `adaptive`, `auto`.
93	3. **Default ship policy** (REQ-F-021): hybrid — adaptive 4-minute idle trigger for short prefixes; auto-switches to 1-hour TTL when prefix size P > 50k tokens.
94	4. Idle-only triggering (REQ-F-020) — never ping when a real turn just touched the cache.
95	
96	**Gate.** Time-mocked integration test that verifies pings fire only when expected.
97	
98	---

100	### M7 — MCP Server + CLI  *(depends on M6)*
101	
102	**Spec:** [designs/06-systems-design.md:367](designs/06-systems-design.md); CLI surface at [designs/03-engineering-specs.md:159–172](designs/03-engineering-specs.md).
103	
104	**Goal.** Wire up the user-facing surface area. Until this milestone, Cachelane works but has no way to install/inspect/configure from a terminal.
105	
106	**Deliverables.**
107	1. **MCP tools** (stdio transport, no network port — REQ-F-001):
108	   - `cachelane:stats` — reads SQLite, prints cache-hit ratio, token reduction, effective cost.
109	   - `cachelane:explain` — explains why a given turn was pruned/reordered the way it was.
110	   - `cachelane:expand` — already partly built in M5; wire the MCP tool surface here.
111	2. **CLI commands** (`commander`):
112	   ```
113	   cachelane install / uninstall / enable / disable / doctor
114	   cachelane stats [--since <ISO-duration>] [--opt-in]
115	   cachelane explain [--turn <N>]
116	   cachelane pin <file|glob> / exclude <file|glob>
117	   cachelane prune --aggressive | --conservative | --default
118	   cachelane keepalive <off|static|adaptive|auto>
119	   ```
120	3. **Config file wiring** — `~/.cachelane/config.json` with all knobs exposed (schema at [designs/03-engineering-specs.md:174–184](designs/03-engineering-specs.md)).
121	
122	**Gate.** End-to-end test against a recorded Claude Code session.
